narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which is above the typical student's 3.21, and an LSAT score of 46, well ahead of the average 36.70—those are standout achievements that highlight your potential. You're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of students in our dataset, and come from an upper-middle income family, which is a solid foundation. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 60.5% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, both at 3—below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5—suggesting your early and later undergrad performance placed you in the lower third of your peers, which weighed heavily against you. Your LSAT, on the other hand, strongly pushed the model toward predicting success, counterbalancing some concerns. Your solid GPA offered a small boost in the same direction, while family income and full-time status had minimal sway. Compared to typical students who passed the bar, your deciles are noticeably lower, which amplified the risk in the model's eyes despite your other highs. This isn't a judgment on your drive or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers—it's just how these academic rankings stacked up. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as those proved most pivotal here, and reapply with even stronger evidence of consistent performance. You have the talent; refining those early hurdles could flip the script next time. We're rooting for you.
